subject_profile.summary: Highly educated American, board-certified laboratory animal veterinarian (ACLAM), considering relocation.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Scores predate full v2 source-discipline re-run.
Minor arithmetic note: institutional composite recomputed at +4.17 (originally reported +4.2).

economic.notes: Better starting fiscal position than US; strong central-bank and banking institutions; main drag is involuntary trade exposure to US actions.

fiscal_state.notes: Deficit ~2.1% GDP declining to 1.4% by 2030-31; lowest debt-to-GDP in G7; Carney investment-led growth with planned operational cuts.

monetary_independence.notes: Bank of Canada strong independence; no political pressure campaign; inflation target intact.

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: CAD not a reserve currency; factor effectively neutral.

inflation.notes: Near 2% target; less oil-shock exposure (own production); housing cost structural problem.

banking_stability.notes: Big Six oligopoly, strong capital, conservative lending, robust OSFI.

trade_policy.notes: Heavy involuntary US tariff exposure under USMCA; diversifying toward EU/Asia; adaptation assumed over time.

institutional.notes: Strong across the board; among top-15 globally on rule of law.

rule_of_law.notes: Independent, respected Supreme Court; notwithstanding-clause friction operates within rule-of-law framework; WJP top 15.

statistical_integrity.notes: Statistics Canada highly regarded; no documented interference.

civil_service_capacity.notes: Major restructuring; Carney planned 7.5-15% operational cuts on normal timelines.

press_freedom.notes: Consistently high rankings; CBC under pressure but operating; some consolidation concerns.

political_social.notes: Well below US polarization; immigration less welcoming than 5y ago but orderly. Long-term composite ~+1.94 as demographics soften.

political_stability.notes: Carney Liberal minority since April 2025; stable in operation; peaceful transfers the norm.

civil_liberties.notes: Charter protections; some concerns (Online Streaming Act, C-63, 2022 Emergencies Act use) within judicial oversight.

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Immigration targets cut, student caps, TFW restricted, PR narrowed; but due process maintained, no mass deportation, system within rule of law.

social_cohesion.notes: Rising polarization from higher baseline; Quebec and Indigenous tensions managed; limited political violence.

demographics.notes: Growth was immigration-driven, now declining due to cuts; below-replacement native fertility; aging.

geopolitical.notes: Strong on conflict-avoidance and sanctions safety; negative only on alliance reliability due to direct US targeting.

alliance_reliability.notes: Five Eyes degraded (Canada specifically targeted for removal); US tariff warfare; pivoting to Canada-Nordic frameworks, EU; AUKUS exclusion painful but adapting.

conflict_involvement.notes: No active conflicts; limited NATO obligations; Arctic contested but not militarized; no Iran involvement.

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Follows Western sanctions; not a major imposer; no capital controls; some secondary exposure to US actions.

physical_practical.notes: Universal healthcare with access problems but better aggregate outcomes than US; very low violent crime.

climate.notes: Net beneficiary in parts (northern agriculture, heating); wildfire and Arctic/permafrost exposure; BC/Ontario favored.

healthcare.notes: Universal single-payer; primary-care shortages and wait times; lower cost, better outcomes than US.

infrastructure.notes: Adequate; housing infrastructure investment planned; good transit in major cities; strong broadband.

crime_safety.notes: Homicide ~1/3 of US; firearm violence vastly lower; mass shootings rare.

disaster_insurance.notes: Rising wildfire/flood in regions but insurance markets functional; federal response intact.

personal_fit.notes: Highest-feasibility option. Active vet shortage incl. lab animal medicine; AAALAC facilities at every major institution; Express Entry fast and direct; ACLAM fully recognized; smoothest cultural transition. Salaries lower (CAD 130-220K).

labanimal_vet_fit.notes: Open positions at Toronto, McGill, UBC, McMaster, Alberta, Calgary, Saskatchewan, Guelph; provincial licensure main hurdle; ~12-18 month realistic timeline.

summary: Highest-feasibility strong option for the subject. Strong institutions (+4.17), positive political/social state, excellent personal fit (active vet shortage, fast Express Entry, full ACLAM recognition). Main weaknesses are involuntary US trade exposure and degraded alliance position from being directly targeted by the US. Roughly tied with Sweden once personal fit is included; the choice between them is preference-driven.
